title: Respiratory Failure — Type 1
oneLiner: Hypoxaemic respiratory failure (PaO₂ <8 kPa) with normal/low PaCO₂; caused by V/Q mismatch or shunt; common in pneumonia, PE, pulmonary oedema.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Tension pneumothorax:** Absent breath sounds + hyper-resonant percussion + tracheal deviation + hypotension → immediate decompression (needle thoracostomy, 2nd intercostal space mid-clavicular line) *before* any imaging.
- **Massive pulmonary embolism:** Acute-onset dyspnoea, pleuritic pain, raised JVP, clear chest, + right heart strain on ECG (S₁Q₃T₃, RV strain) → call for thrombolysis (e.g. alteplase) if shocked; do not wait for CTPA if high suspicion.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Acute or subacute dyspnoea, tachypnoea, dry cough (or productive if pneumonia), pleuritic chest pain (if PE), confusion/agitation (severe hypoxia).
- **Key exam findings:** Tachypnoea (>20/min), accessory muscle use, intercostal/subcostal recession, central cyanosis (late), hypoxia on pulse oximetry (SpO₂ <94% on room air), tachycardia ± hypotension.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Pneumonia** – fever, purulent sputum, focal crackles/consolidation on CXR
  - **Pulmonary oedema (cardiogenic)** – orthopnoea, PND, raised JVP, bibasal crackles, third heart sound, bilateral peri-hilar infiltrates on CXR
  - **Pulmonary embolism** – acute onset, pleuritic pain, haemoptysis, raised JVP, normal CXR, risk factors (surgery, OCP, immobility)
  - **ARDS** – bilateral infiltrates, no JVP, normal CXR initially, history of sepsis/pancreatitis/trauma
  - **Atelectasis** – post-operative, dull percussion, decreased breath sounds, resolves with deep breathing
  - **Interstitial lung disease exacerbation** – known ILD, diffuse fine crackles, HRCT pattern
- **Splitter:** Lung ultrasound + clinical context – unilobar consolidation + fever = pneumonia; bilateral B-lines + high JVP = pulmonary oedema; unilateral absent gliding + point of lung point = pneumothorax; RV strain on echo + clear lungs = PE.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):** Pulse oximetry, ABG (confirm PaO₂ <8 kPa, PaCO₂ normal/low, pH normal/alkalotic), ECG (sinus tachycardia, RV strain in PE, ischaemia), lung ultrasound (B-lines, consolidation, pleural effusion, pneumothorax).
- **Bloods:** FBC, U+E, CRP, procalcitonin (if pneumonia), troponin (PE, ACS), NT-proBNP (pulmonary oedema), D-dimer (if low/intermediate Wells for PE), blood cultures (if febrile).
- **Imaging:** First-line – CXR (AP or PA) STAT. Second-line – CT pulmonary angiogram (if PE suspected, GFR >30) or CT chest (if ARDS, interstitial disease). V/Q scan if CT contraindicated (e.g. contrast allergy, pregnancy).
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Type 1 respiratory failure – PaO₂ <8 kPa (60 mmHg) on room air, PaCO₂ <6 kPa (45 mmHg). ARDS Berlin criteria: PaO₂/FiO₂ <300 (mild), <200 (moderate), <100 (severe) with bilateral opacities and no evidence of cardiogenic oedema.
- **Never-miss test:** **Arterial blood gas (ABG)** – to confirm type 1 vs type 2, quantify severity, and guide oxygen therapy. Omission risks missing hypercapnia (type 2) or underestimating hypoxia.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Oxygen:** Target SpO₂ 94–98% (BTS). Start 15 L/min via non-rebreather mask if severe hypoxia; titrate down to maintain target. *In confirmed type 1, high FiO₂ is safe – no risk of CO₂ retention.*
  - **Position:** Sit upright (if not shocked) to improve ventilation/perfusion matching.
  - **Treat underlying cause:**
    - *Pneumonia* – antibiotics (see Drugs & Doses).
    - *PE* – if massive (shock) → thrombolysis; otherwise LMWH.
    - *Pulmonary oedema* – IV furosemide ± nitrates (if BP adequate) – see Drugs.
    - *Pneumothorax* – needle decompression then chest drain.
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - Monitor SpO₂, respiratory rate, heart rate, BP, temperature, urine output.
  - Consider NIV (CPAP) if PaO₂ <8 kPa despite FiO₂ ≥50% (e.g. in pulmonary oedema, pneumonia with severe shunt).
  - Repeat ABG if clinical deterioration.
  - Determine aetiology and start specific treatment (e.g. antibiotics, anticoagulation, diuretics, steroids for ARDS? [check local policy – steroids not routine in all causes]).
  - Physiotherapy for atelectasis, incentive spirometry.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - If hypoxia persists after resolution of acute cause → assess for home oxygen (criteria: PaO₂ <7.3 kPa on two occasions >3 weeks apart in stable state).
  - Pulmonary rehabilitation if post-infective or ILD.
  - Follow-up CXR at 6–8 weeks for pneumonia (to rule out malignancy).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% saline at 1 ml/kg/h (or [check local policy]); be cautious in pulmonary oedema – restrict.
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless contraindicated (bleeding, co-agulopathy).
  - Diet: oral as tolerated (if not ventilated), NBM only if intubated or acutely unwell.
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly (30 minutes if unstable).
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Oxygen:** via nasal cannulae (1–4 L/min) or non-rebreather (10–15 L/min). Titrate to SpO₂ 94–98%.
  - **Pneumonia (CAP, starting IV):** Amoxicillin 500 mg IV TDS *or* doxycycline 200 mg PO/IV on day 1, then 100 mg OD (if penicillin allergy). Add clarithromycin 500 mg IV BD if severe CAP (per NICE). [Check local policy – doses vary.]
  - **Pulmonary oedema:** Furosemide 40–80 mg IV slowly (over 2–3 min); can repeat after 30 min if poor response. Check renal function first.
  - **PE (massive with shock):** Alteplase 10 mg IV bolus, then 90 mg infused over 2 hours (contraindicated if recent major bleeding). Non-massive: Enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg SC OD (or 1 mg/kg BD if high bleeding risk) – [check local policy].
- **Common alternatives:** For pneumonia – levofloxacin 500 mg IV/PO BD (if allergy or atypical). For pulmonary oedema – bumetanide 1–2 mg IV. For PE – fondaparinux 7.5 mg SC OD (if HIT history).
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Furosemide → avoid in severe hyponatraemia, anuria; monitor K+.
  - LMWH → avoid if active bleeding, coagulopathy; use half-dose if eGFR <30.
  - Doxycycline → avoid in children <12 years; reduce dose in hepatic impairment.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Furosemide – lower doses if renal failure (but may need higher to achieve effect – [check local policy]).
  - LMWH – enoxaparin dose reduced if eGFR <30 (1 mg/kg BD or [check local policy]).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Oxygen** – though safe in type 1, never give high-flow O₂ to a patient with *type 2* respiratory failure (e.g. COPD) without controlled support. Always check ABG first. For diuretics – giving IV furosemide without checking K+ can cause arrhythmias.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Worsening hypoxia → respiratory arrest, multi-organ failure (e.g. AKI, hepatic dysfunction), arrhythmias, intubation requirement.
- **Chronic:** If untreated/recurrent – pulmonary hypertension, cor pulmonale (right heart failure), fibrotic changes (e.g. post-ARDS).
sections.Prognosis: - Varies by aetiology: Pneumonia (mortality 5–15% for CAP, higher if elderly); Massive PE (in-hospital mortality ~30% if shocked); ARDS (mortality 40–60% depending on severity). Recovery often complete if cause reversed early, but residual impairment (fibrosis, muscle weakness) may occur.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check an ABG** in any hypoxic patient to classify respiratory failure and guide oxygen therapy.
- **Calculate PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio** (P/F ratio) – a value <300 defines ARDS and helps risk stratify.
- **Use lung ultrasound** at the bedside to quickly differentiate between pneumonia, PE, pulmonary oedema, and pneumothorax (with practice).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Giving high-flow oxygen without confirming type 1 failure** – in an undifferentiated breathless patient, if they have type 2 (COPD), you may suppress respiratory drive.
- **Treating hypoxia without looking for the cause** – oxygen alone delays diagnosis of PE, pneumothorax, or pulmonary oedema.
- **Sitting a hypotensive patient upright** – if they are shocked (e.g. massive PE), positional reduction in preload can cause cardiac arrest – lay flat.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Worsening breathlessness, fever not settling, chest pain, coughing up blood, or feeling generally unwell.
- **Follow-up:** General practice within 48 h for mild cases; for pneumonia, repeat CXR at 6–8 weeks (if >50 years or smoker) to exclude underlying malignancy.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU if:** NEWS2 ≥5, SpO₂ <90% despite 40% FiO₂, respiratory rate >30/min, lactate >2 mmol/L, GCS <15, hypotension (systolic <90 mmHg), rising PaCO₂, or patient unable to clear secretions.
- **Specific triggers:** P/F ratio <200 despite FiO₂ >0.5, need for NIV/CPAP, rising oxygen requirement, or signs of multi-organ failure.

rawMarkdown:
## Respiratory Failure — Type 1

**One-liner:** Hypoxaemic respiratory failure (PaO₂ <8 kPa) with normal/low PaCO₂; caused by V/Q mismatch or shunt; common in pneumonia, PE, pulmonary oedema.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Tension pneumothorax:** Absent breath sounds + hyper-resonant percussion + tracheal deviation + hypotension → immediate decompression (needle thoracostomy, 2nd intercostal space mid-clavicular line) *before* any imaging.
- **Massive pulmonary embolism:** Acute-onset dyspnoea, pleuritic pain, raised JVP, clear chest, + right heart strain on ECG (S₁Q₃T₃, RV strain) → call for thrombolysis (e.g. alteplase) if shocked; do not wait for CTPA if high suspicion.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Acute or subacute dyspnoea, tachypnoea, dry cough (or productive if pneumonia), pleuritic chest pain (if PE), confusion/agitation (severe hypoxia).
- **Key exam findings:** Tachypnoea (>20/min), accessory muscle use, intercostal/subcostal recession, central cyanosis (late), hypoxia on pulse oximetry (SpO₂ <94% on room air), tachycardia ± hypotension.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Pneumonia** – fever, purulent sputum, focal crackles/consolidation on CXR
  - **Pulmonary oedema (cardiogenic)** – orthopnoea, PND, raised JVP, bibasal crackles, third heart sound, bilateral peri-hilar infiltrates on CXR
  - **Pulmonary embolism** – acute onset, pleuritic pain, haemoptysis, raised JVP, normal CXR, risk factors (surgery, OCP, immobility)
  - **ARDS** – bilateral infiltrates, no JVP, normal CXR initially, history of sepsis/pancreatitis/trauma
  - **Atelectasis** – post-operative, dull percussion, decreased breath sounds, resolves with deep breathing
  - **Interstitial lung disease exacerbation** – known ILD, diffuse fine crackles, HRCT pattern
- **Splitter:** Lung ultrasound + clinical context – unilobar consolidation + fever = pneumonia; bilateral B-lines + high JVP = pulmonary oedema; unilateral absent gliding + point of lung point = pneumothorax; RV strain on echo + clear lungs = PE.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):** Pulse oximetry, ABG (confirm PaO₂ <8 kPa, PaCO₂ normal/low, pH normal/alkalotic), ECG (sinus tachycardia, RV strain in PE, ischaemia), lung ultrasound (B-lines, consolidation, pleural effusion, pneumothorax).
- **Bloods:** FBC, U+E, CRP, procalcitonin (if pneumonia), troponin (PE, ACS), NT-proBNP (pulmonary oedema), D-dimer (if low/intermediate Wells for PE), blood cultures (if febrile).
- **Imaging:** First-line – CXR (AP or PA) STAT. Second-line – CT pulmonary angiogram (if PE suspected, GFR >30) or CT chest (if ARDS, interstitial disease). V/Q scan if CT contraindicated (e.g. contrast allergy, pregnancy).
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Type 1 respiratory failure – PaO₂ <8 kPa (60 mmHg) on room air, PaCO₂ <6 kPa (45 mmHg). ARDS Berlin criteria: PaO₂/FiO₂ <300 (mild), <200 (moderate), <100 (severe) with bilateral opacities and no evidence of cardiogenic oedema.
- **Never-miss test:** **Arterial blood gas (ABG)** – to confirm type 1 vs type 2, quantify severity, and guide oxygen therapy. Omission risks missing hypercapnia (type 2) or underestimating hypoxia.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Oxygen:** Target SpO₂ 94–98% (BTS). Start 15 L/min via non-rebreather mask if severe hypoxia; titrate down to maintain target. *In confirmed type 1, high FiO₂ is safe – no risk of CO₂ retention.*
  - **Position:** Sit upright (if not shocked) to improve ventilation/perfusion matching.
  - **Treat underlying cause:**
    - *Pneumonia* – antibiotics (see Drugs & Doses).
    - *PE* – if massive (shock) → thrombolysis; otherwise LMWH.
    - *Pulmonary oedema* – IV furosemide ± nitrates (if BP adequate) – see Drugs.
    - *Pneumothorax* – needle decompression then chest drain.
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - Monitor SpO₂, respiratory rate, heart rate, BP, temperature, urine output.
  - Consider NIV (CPAP) if PaO₂ <8 kPa despite FiO₂ ≥50% (e.g. in pulmonary oedema, pneumonia with severe shunt).
  - Repeat ABG if clinical deterioration.
  - Determine aetiology and start specific treatment (e.g. antibiotics, anticoagulation, diuretics, steroids for ARDS? [check local policy – steroids not routine in all causes]).
  - Physiotherapy for atelectasis, incentive spirometry.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - If hypoxia persists after resolution of acute cause → assess for home oxygen (criteria: PaO₂ <7.3 kPa on two occasions >3 weeks apart in stable state).
  - Pulmonary rehabilitation if post-infective or ILD.
  - Follow-up CXR at 6–8 weeks for pneumonia (to rule out malignancy).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% saline at 1 ml/kg/h (or [check local policy]); be cautious in pulmonary oedema – restrict.
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless contraindicated (bleeding, co-agulopathy).
  - Diet: oral as tolerated (if not ventilated), NBM only if intubated or acutely unwell.
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly (30 minutes if unstable).

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Oxygen:** via nasal cannulae (1–4 L/min) or non-rebreather (10–15 L/min). Titrate to SpO₂ 94–98%.
  - **Pneumonia (CAP, starting IV):** Amoxicillin 500 mg IV TDS *or* doxycycline 200 mg PO/IV on day 1, then 100 mg OD (if penicillin allergy). Add clarithromycin 500 mg IV BD if severe CAP (per NICE). [Check local policy – doses vary.]
  - **Pulmonary oedema:** Furosemide 40–80 mg IV slowly (over 2–3 min); can repeat after 30 min if poor response. Check renal function first.
  - **PE (massive with shock):** Alteplase 10 mg IV bolus, then 90 mg infused over 2 hours (contraindicated if recent major bleeding). Non-massive: Enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg SC OD (or 1 mg/kg BD if high bleeding risk) – [check local policy].
- **Common alternatives:** For pneumonia – levofloxacin 500 mg IV/PO BD (if allergy or atypical). For pulmonary oedema – bumetanide 1–2 mg IV. For PE – fondaparinux 7.5 mg SC OD (if HIT history).
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Furosemide → avoid in severe hyponatraemia, anuria; monitor K+.
  - LMWH → avoid if active bleeding, coagulopathy; use half-dose if eGFR <30.
  - Doxycycline → avoid in children <12 years; reduce dose in hepatic impairment.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Furosemide – lower doses if renal failure (but may need higher to achieve effect – [check local policy]).
  - LMWH – enoxaparin dose reduced if eGFR <30 (1 mg/kg BD or [check local policy]).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Oxygen** – though safe in type 1, never give high-flow O₂ to a patient with *type 2* respiratory failure (e.g. COPD) without controlled support. Always check ABG first. For diuretics – giving IV furosemide without checking K+ can cause arrhythmias.

### Complications
- **Acute:** Worsening hypoxia → respiratory arrest, multi-organ failure (e.g. AKI, hepatic dysfunction), arrhythmias, intubation requirement.
- **Chronic:** If untreated/recurrent – pulmonary hypertension, cor pulmonale (right heart failure), fibrotic changes (e.g. post-ARDS).

### Prognosis
- Varies by aetiology: Pneumonia (mortality 5–15% for CAP, higher if elderly); Massive PE (in-hospital mortality ~30% if shocked); ARDS (mortality 40–60% depending on severity). Recovery often complete if cause reversed early, but residual impairment (fibrosis, muscle weakness) may occur.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check an ABG** in any hypoxic patient to classify respiratory failure and guide oxygen therapy.
- **Calculate PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio** (P/F ratio) – a value <300 defines ARDS and helps risk stratify.
- **Use lung ultrasound** at the bedside to quickly differentiate between pneumonia, PE, pulmonary oedema, and pneumothorax (with practice).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Giving high-flow oxygen without confirming type 1 failure** – in an undifferentiated breathless patient, if they have type 2 (COPD), you may suppress respiratory drive.
- **Treating hypoxia without looking for the cause** – oxygen alone delays diagnosis of PE, pneumothorax, or pulmonary oedema.
- **Sitting a hypotensive patient upright** – if they are shocked (e.g. massive PE), positional reduction in preload can cause cardiac arrest – lay flat.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Worsening breathlessness, fever not settling, chest pain, coughing up blood, or feeling generally unwell.
- **Follow-up:** General practice within 48 h for mild cases; for pneumonia, repeat CXR at 6–8 weeks (if >50 years or smoker) to exclude underlying malignancy.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU if:** NEWS2 ≥5, SpO₂ <90% despite 40% FiO₂, respiratory rate >30/min, lactate >2 mmol/L, GCS <15, hypotension (systolic <90 mmHg), rising PaCO₂, or patient unable to clear secretions.
- **Specific triggers:** P/F ratio <200 despite FiO₂ >0.5, need for NIV/CPAP, rising oxygen requirement, or signs of multi-organ failure.
